perturbation.original_text: (b) Except as otherwise expressly provided in this Agreement, Service Provider shall perform the Services and its obligations under this Agreement, and act at all times as an independent Service Provider of Owner. None of Service Provider's employees shall be, or shall be considered to be, employees of Owner. Service Provider shall be fully responsible for the payment of all wages, salaries, benefits and other compensation to its employees.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Except as otherwise expressly provided in this Agreement, Service Provider shall perform the Services and its obligations under this Agreement, and act at all times as an independent Service Provider of Owner. None of Service Provider's personnel shall be, or shall be considered to be, employees of Owner. Service Provider shall only be responsible for the payment of wages, and Owner will handle all salaries, benefits and other compensation to Service Provider's employees.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the 'Owner will handle all salaries, benefits, and other compensation,' this contradicts the independent contractor status, suggesting an employer-employee relationship where the owner controls compensation, a key factor in determining employee status.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Labor Code Section 3351 defines 'employee' and the rights and responsibilities tied to that classification. Misclassifying an employee as an independent contractor can lead to violations of wage and hour laws, tax laws, and workers' compensation laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code defines employees and employers based on control over wages, hours, and working conditions. The modified clause contradicts this by having the Owner control compensation, suggesting an employment relationship rather than an independent contractor arrangement. This affects obligations for taxes, insurance, and labor law compliance.

WORKERS' COMPENSATION AND INSURANCE [3200 - 6002] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1979, Ch. 373. ) PART 1. SCOPE AND OPERATION [3200 - 4418] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employers, Employees, and Dependents [3300 - 3553] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 2. Employees [3350 - 3371.1] ( Article 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 3351. “Employee” means every person in the service of an employer under any appointment or contract of hire or apprenticeship, express or implied, oral or written, whether lawfully or unlawfully employed, and includes: (a) Persons who are not citizens or nationals of the United States and minors. (b) All elected and appointed paid public officers. (c) All officers and members of boards of directors of quasi-public or private corporations while rendering actual service for the corporations for pay. An officer or member of a board of directors may elect to be excluded from coverage in accordance with paragraph (16), (18), or (19) of subdivision (a) of Section 3352. (d) Except as provided in paragraph (8) of subdivision (a) of Section 3352, any person employed by the owner or occupant of a residential dwelling whose duties are incidental to the ownership, mainten
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original agreement by shifting the responsibility for salaries, benefits, and other compensation to the Owner, which undermines the independent contractor status of the Service Provider. Scraped Law Snippet 1, LAB 3351, defines 'employee' as 'every person in the service of an employer under any appointment or contract of hire,' which the modified text implies by having the Owner handle compensation. This contradicts the original intent of the agreement, where the Service Provider was fully responsible for all employee compensation, reinforcing their independent contractor status. The 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' arises because the altered compensation arrangement suggests an employer-employee relationship, deviating from the legally recognized criteria for independent contractors.

perturbation.original_text: 1.7 Personnel Standards. (a) Service Provider's employees shall be qualified (and if required by applicable law, licensed, certified or registered) and experienced in the functions to which they are assigned and shall meet the requirements of all permits, all applicable laws and the then-current SEF maintenance manuals (to the extent copies of which have been provided to Service Provider by Owner).
perturbation.changed_text: 1.7 Personnel Standards. (a) Service Provider's employees shall be qualified (and if required by applicable law, licensed, certified or registered). The level of experience shall be determined at the discretion of the Service Provider, and they shall substantially comply with the requirements of all permits, all applicable laws and the then-current SEF maintenance manuals (to the extent copies of which have been provided to Service Provider by Owner).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall meet' to 'substantially comply' weakens the compliance requirement, creating ambiguity about whether the personnel truly meet legal and regulatory requirements, potentially affecting safety and regulatory adherence.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Regulations, Title 8, section 3203 regarding Injury and Illness Prevention Programs requires employers to ensure employees are adequately trained and qualified for their tasks to maintain a safe working environment.
perturbation.law_explanation: The altered text weakens the requirement for personnel to 'meet' all permit and legal requirements, replacing it with 'substantially comply.' This directly contradicts the spirit and intent of California's safety regulations, which mandates full compliance to ensure worker safety and prevent workplace hazards. The altered text introduces ambiguity and could lead to inadequate training or qualifications, violating the regulation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code of Regulations, Title 8, Section 3203. Injury and Illness Prevention Program. Skip to Main Content This information is provided free of charge by the Department of Industrial Relations from its web site at www.dir.ca.gov . These regulations are for the convenience of the user and no representation or warranty is made that the information is current or accurate. See full disclaimer at https://www.dir.ca.gov/od_pub/disclaimer.html . Subchapter 7. General Industry Safety Orders Group 1. General Physical Conditions and Structures Orders Introduction Return to index New query §3203. Injury and Illness Prevention Program. (a) Effective July 1, 1991, every employer shall establish, implement and maintain an effective Injury and Illness Prevention Program (Program). The Program shall be in writing and, shall, at a minimum: (1) Identify the person or persons with authority and responsibility for implementing the Program. (2) Include a system for ensuring that employees comply with safe and healthy work practices. Substantial compliance with this provision includes recognition of employees who follow safe and healthful work practices, training and retraining programs, disciplinary actions, or any other such means that ensures employee compliance with safe and healthful work practices. (3) Include a system for communicating with employees in a form readily understandable by all affected employees on matters relating to occupational safety and health, including provisions designed to encourage employees to inform the employer of hazards at the worksite without fear of reprisal. Substantial compliance with this provision includes meetings, training programs, posting, written communications, a system of anonymous notification by employees about hazards, labor/management safety and health committees, or any other means that ensures communication with employees. Exception: Employers having fewer than 10 employees shall be permitted to communicate to and instruct employees orally in general safe work practices with spe
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - California Code of Regulations This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates that the service provider's employees 'shall meet' the requirements of all permits and applicable laws. The changed text weakens this requirement by stating that they 'shall substantially comply' with the same requirements. This introduces ambiguity, as 'substantially comply' does not guarantee full compliance with applicable laws, potentially resulting in a deviation from legal and regulatory standards. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Code of Regulations, Title 8, Section 3203, addresses the Injury and Illness Prevention Program and includes a system for ensuring that employees comply with safe and healthy work practices. While the snippet uses the term 'substantial compliance' within the context of methods ensuring employee compliance, the altered contract language uses 'substantially comply' as the *standard* of compliance itself. This distinction is critical. The original text aligns with a strict interpretation of legal compliance, while the altered text allows for a potentially lower standard, creating a legal contradiction because it does not ensure employees fully meet legal standards and may violate the intent of regulations requiring full compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 10.1 Hazardous Conditions. Service Provider is not responsible for any Hazardous Conditions encountered at the Premises. Upon encountering any Hazardous Conditions, Service Provider will stop work immediately in the affected area and duly notify Owner and, if required by any legal requirements, all government or quasi-government entities with jurisdiction over the Premises.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 Hazardous Conditions. Service Provider is not responsible for any Hazardous Conditions encountered at the Premises. Upon encountering any Hazardous Conditions, Service Provider will continue work in the affected area with caution and notify Owner within 72 hours, unless instructed otherwise by the owner.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the response to hazardous conditions from immediate cessation to continued work with delayed notification creates a direct conflict with standard safety regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 29 CFR 1910.1200(h) Hazard Communication requires immediate action when hazardous materials are encountered, including employee training, proper handling, and immediate reporting to ensure safety and compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: OSHA's Hazard Communication standard mandates immediate action to protect employees upon encountering hazardous conditions. The modified text directly contradicts this by allowing work to continue and delaying notification, potentially exposing workers to harm and violating federal safety regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication. Part Number: 1910 Part Number Title: Occupational Safety and Health Standards Standard Number: 1910.1200 Title: Hazard Communication. Appendix: A B C D E F GPO Source: e-CFR 1910.1200(a) Purpose . 1910.1200(a)(1) The purpose of this section is to ensure that the hazards of all chemicals produced or imported are classified, and that information concerning the classified hazards is transmitted to employers and employees. The requirements of this section are intended to be consistent with the provisions of the United Nations Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labeling of Chemicals (GHS), primarily Revision 7. The transmittal of information is to be accomplished by means of comprehensive hazard communication programs, which are to include container labeling and other forms of warning, safety data sheets and employee training. 1910.1200(a)(2) This occupational safety and health standard is intended to address comprehensively the issue of classifying the potential hazards of chemicals, and communicating information concerning hazards and appropriate protective measures to employees, and to preempt any legislative or regulatory enactments of a state, or political subdivision of a state, pertaining to this subject. Classifying the potential hazards of chemicals and communicating information concerning hazards and appropriate protective measures to employees, may include, for example, but is not limited to, provisions for: developing and maintaining a written hazard communication program for the workplace, including lists of hazardous chemicals present; labeling of containers of chemicals in the workplace, as well as of containers of chemicals being shipped to other workplaces; preparation and distribution of safety data sheets to employees and downstream employers; and development and implementation of employee

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the OSHA's Hazard Communication standard. The original text mandates stopping work immediately upon encountering hazardous conditions, whereas the changed text allows for continued work with caution and delayed notification. This is a clear non-compliance, as it weakens the immediate protective measures required by safety regulations. The Law Explanation states that the modified text allows work to continue and delays notification, potentially exposing workers to harm and violating federal safety regulations. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly state 'immediate action', they emphasize the importance of hazard communication and appropriate protective measures, which the modified text undermines by delaying action and notification, thus contradicting the intent of the law.
